coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots with excellent peroxidase mimicking property for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruilin
Liu, Lei
Li, Wei
Luo, Xiaogang
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: The luminescent carbon dots with peroxidase mimicking property had attracted considerable attention in biomedical field. In this work, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra (4-borate phenyl)−21H, 23H-porphyrin Fe (II) (Fe-TBPP) as precursor. The obtained Fe-CDs emitted intense blue luminescence under ultraviolet light irradiation. Moreover, the Fe-CDs exhibited remarkable peroxidase mimicking property, which can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). More importantly, the emission of Fe-CDs could be gradually quenched with the addition of H2O2. Based on these phenomena, a new optical dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose was successfully established. The detection limits of glucose were calculated to be 3.86 and 7.27 μM (S/N = 3) respectively based on the colorimetric and fluorometric methods. Furthermore, we combined this dual-mode detection method with smartphone imaging. The colorimetric and fluorescent images were collected by recognition software of smartphone, which were then transformed into the corresponding HSL values for quantitative determination of glucose. Finally, the dual-mode approach based on Fe-CDs was used for the detection of glucose content in human serum, demonstrating the potential application of carbon dots in the biological area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Porphyrin
Glucose
H2O2
Peroxidase mimetic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Fluorometric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Detection of glucose in the real samples
Optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of glucose

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2022CFC034 ), Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientific Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities of Hubei Province: “Biomass chemical technologies and materials” (grant no. T201908 ), the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (grant no. 2022BEEA06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Fe-CDs (Inset: size distribution of Fe-CDs); (b) HRTEM image of Fe-CDs; (c) XRD of Fe-CDs; (d) FTIR spectra of Fe-TBPP and Fe-CDs; (e) UV-Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of Fe-CDs (Insets: photographs of Fe-CDs solution under daylight and excitation of 365 nm; (f) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (from 280 nm to 440 nm).

Peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs. (a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of the various solutions as stated (Inset: the corresponding sample image); (b-f) Effect of various variables on the mimetic enzymes activity of CDs: (b) pH, (c) temperature, (d) Time, (e) TMB concentration, (f) H2O2 concentration.

(a) Calibration curve of the absorbance versus the glucose concentration under optimal conditions (Insert: images of the system with various glucose concentrations); (b) The linear relationship between absorbance and glucose concentration; (c) Absorbance at 652 nm of the system with various carbohydrates (Inset: images of the system with the corresponding substances; from left to right: glucose, maltose, fructose, lactose and sucrose); (d) Absorbance at 652 nm of the system containing different interferents (sodium ion, potassium ion, magnesium ion, zinc ion, chloride ion, carbonate ion, sulfate ion, isoleucine, glycine, valine, Lysine, Cysteine, Alanine, Arginine and Histidine).

(a) Calibration curve of the ∆F versus the glucose concentration under optimal conditions (Insert: fluorescenceimages of the system with various glucose concentrations under 365 nm laser irradiation); (b) The linear relationship between ∆F and glucose concentration; (c) The ∆F of the system with various carbohydrates (Inset: fluorescence images of the system with the corresponding substances under 365 nm laser irradiation; from left to right: glucose, maltose, fructose, lactose and sucrose); (d) The ∆F of the system with various interferents (sodium ion, potassium ion, magnesium ion, zinc ion, chloride ion, carbonate ion, sulfate ion, isoleucine, glycine, valine, Lysine, Cysteine, Alanine, Arginine and Histidine). ∆F = (F0-F)/F0, F0 and F are defined as the fluorescence intensity before and after adding the test substance, respectively.

(a) The Fe-CDs for glucose detection by smartphone imaging; Calibration curves created by ploting (b) H/L or (c) H/H0 against glucose concentration; (d and e) Smartphone imaging pictures and corresponding HSL average values.

(a) The impact of various scavengers on the relative activity of the Fe-CDs/TMB/H2O2 system; (b) The impact of various scavengers on ∆F of the Fe-CDs/H2O2 system (∆F = (F0-F)/F0, F0 and F are defined as the fluorescence intensity before and after adding the test substance, respectively); (c) EPR spectrum of Fe-CDs samples; (d) Mechanism of glucose detection by Fe-CDs.
Comparison of different glucose detection materials.
Determination of glucose in human serums.

row.entry: Detection result from a glucose meter (mM)

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots with excellent peroxidase mimicking property for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of glucose

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The luminescent carbon dots with peroxidase mimicking property had attracted considerable attention in biomedical field. In this work, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra (4-borate phenyl)−21H, 23H-porphyrin Fe (II) (Fe-TBPP) as precursor. The obtained Fe-CDs emitted intense blue luminescence under ultraviolet light irradiation. Moreover, the Fe-CDs exhibited remarkable peroxidase mimicking property, which can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). More importantly, the emission of Fe-CDs could be gradually quenched with the addition of H2O2. Based on these phenomena, a new optical dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose was successfully established. The detection limits of glucose were calculated to be 3.86 and 7.27 μM (S/N = 3) respectively based on the colorimetric and fluorometric methods. Furthermore, we combined this dual-mode detection method with smartphone imaging. The colorimetric and fluorescent images were collected by recognition software of smartphone, which were then transformed into the corresponding HSL values for quantitative determination of glucose. Finally, the dual-mode approach based on Fe-CDs was used for the detection of glucose content in human serum, demonstrating the potential application of carbon dots in the biological area.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an indispensable component of human beings, glucose provides the energy for cell metabolism and ensures the normal work of body functions. However, the excess of glucose will induce diabetes, as well as common metabolic diseases such as hypertension, heart disease, vision loss, and renal failure. At present, diabetes mellitus has become one of the biggest public health threats [1]. Thus, it is of great significant to develop an efficient method for quantitative determination of glucose in blood serum. To date, many methods for glucose detection had been reported, such as fluorometric [2], colorimetric [3], electrochemical [4], and photoelectrochemical methods [5]. Among them, the optical detection method including fluorometric and colorimetric methods had attracted tremendous interest due to their high sensitivity, simplicity, good reproducibility, and economy [6–9]. However, the conventional optical detection method only had a single colorimetric or fluorescent output signal, which was highly susceptible to environmental fluctuations, resulting in the low accuracy. Therefore, it is extremely urgent to develop a new sensing platform for glucose detection through both fluorometric and colorimetric methods [10–12].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as a new kind of luminescent carbon nanomaterial, had gained considerable interest due to their distinctive properties, such as simple fabrication, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, tunable photoluminescence (PL), and excellent biocompatibility [13–16]. Due to their remarkable fluorescence characteristics, CDs were demonstrated to have potential applications in the field of drug delivery, bioimaging, and sensing, such as metal ions [17,18], small molecules [19–21], and tumor marker [22]. In addition to the above characteristics, some CDs exhibited high mimic enzyme activity [23,24], which could overcome the defects of general artificial enzyme mimics, such as expensive manufacturing and maintenance costs, and unstable properties [25,26]. To data, various CDs with different structures and compositions were demonstrated to have different mimic enzyme activities, such as oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activity [27–29]. Generally, the peroxidase-like nano-enzymes can catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can oxidize the colorless substrates to a colorful material [23,30]. Therefore, the fluorescent CDs with mimic enzyme activities had great potential as nano-sensor for the detection of H2O2 and related molecules through dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

Herein, we successfully synthesized the new carbon dots by one-pot hydrothermal method with 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra (4-borate phenyl)−21H, 23H-porphyrin Fe (II) (Fe-TBPP) as precursor. The as-prepared Fe-CDs showed high peroxidase-mimetic activity, which could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2 •-, further oxidizing the colorless 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB). Moreover, Fe-CDs could be used as a selective fluorometric sensor for the detection of H2O2 through the fluorescence “on-off” model. Based on these phenomena, a new optical dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) detection method was established for the detection of H2O2 and glucose based on the as-prepared CDs. Moreover, we combined this dual-mode method with smartphone imaging to realize the detection of glucose content. Finally, the practical feasibility of Fe-CDs for glucose detection was demonstrated in human serum samples using both colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

The synthetic routes of 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra (4-borate phenyl)−21H, 23H-porphyrin Fe (II) (Fe-TBPP) were shown in Scheme S1. Fe-CDs were prepared by a solvothermal method. Briefly, Fe-TBPP (10 mg), concentrated nitric acid (200 μL) and 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA, 150 μL) were dissolved in ethanol (50 mL). The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave After heated at 160 °C for 8 h, the reaction solution was centrifuged to remove the solid precipitate. Finally, the clear solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 48 h followed by lyophilization to yield the product.

The steady-state kinetics of Fe-CDs were evaluated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.8), which included 100 μL of 1 mg/mL Fe-CDs and various concentrations of TMB (0.1–1.4 mM) or H2O2 (0.1–2.5 mM) as substrate. The absorbance changes were recorded at 55 °C with interval of 60 s. The initial velocity at different substrate concentrations were determined by Lambert Beer law (the molar extinction coefficient of ox-TMB at 652 nm was 39,000 M−1 cm−1). V max and K m can be obtained by fitting the substrate concentration and reaction velocity according to the following Michaelis-Menten equation.
1
V
=
K
m
V
m
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
m
Where V represent initial rate of enzyme reaction at
[ S ]
,
[ S ]
refers to the substrate concentration,
V
max
represent the maximum velocity, and
K
m
refers to the Michaelis constant, an essential parameter reflecting the affinities of the enzyme and substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Specifically, 100 μL of Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL), 100 μL of TMB (10 mM), 100 μL of H2O2 at varied concentrations were mixed with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.8). After incubation at 55 ℃ for 10 min, the corresponding UV spectra of the reacted solution were collected. For the glucose detection, 100 μL of glucose at varied concentrations and 100 μL of glucose oxidase were incubated at 37 ℃. Subsequently, the above solution was then mixed with 2.6 mL of buffer (pH = 3.8), 100 μL of Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL), and 100 μL of TMB solution (10 mM). After incubation at 55 ℃ for 10 min, the corresponding absorption spectra of the system was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Specifically, 100 μL of Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL) solution and 100 μL of H2O2 at varied concentrations were mixed with the Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer (pH=10.0). After gently shaking for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission peak at 465 nm (λex =380 nm) was recorded. For the glucose detection, 100 μL of glucose at varied concentrations and 100 μL of glucose oxidase (1 mg/mL) were incubated at 37 ℃. Subsequently, 2.7 mL of buffer (pH=10.0) and 100 μL of Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL) were sequentially added to the above solution. After incubation at 30 ℃ for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission peak of the above reaction mixture was recorded.

The selectivity and anti-interference of Fe-CDs for glucose detection were conducted through a series of control experiments. Specifically, the changes of absorbance or fluorescence intensity were monitored upon the addition of various other saccharides and common metal ions in serum, and a series of amino acids. The final concentration of glucose was set as 1 mM, while others 5 mM.

For colorimetric and fluorometric methods, the color of the reaction solution was photographed using a smartphone camera under natural sunlight and ultraviolet light, respectively. The photos were then recognized by the software (Color Identification) in smartphone to obtain the Hue-Saturation-Lightness (HSL) values. The standard curve of glucose was then obtained using HSL values and the concentration of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For glucose assay in the real biological samples, we selected the sera of two healthy adult volunteers for detection. The samples were firstly centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the large aggregates and then filtered by a 0.22 µm Millipore filter.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to describe the size and morphology of Fe-CDs. As indicated in Fig. 1a, Fe-CDs showed the spherical shape with an average particle size of 2.35 nm. The lattice fringes of Fe-CDs can be observed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As indicated in Fig. 1b, Fe-CDs possessed the typical ordered lattices with an interspace of 0.22 nm, ascribed to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [31]. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Fe-CDs exhibited only one prominent diffraction peak at 21° (Fig. 1c), ascribed to the graphitic structure with spacing (100) [32]. Thus, Fe-CDs demonstrated the high crystallinity and graphitic structure. The FT-IR spectrum was used to verify the functional groups on the surface of Fe-CDs. As indicated in Fig. 1d, the peak around 3435 cm−1 was ascribed to the hydroxyl group (–OH), and the sharp bands at 2924 and 2849 cm−1 could be assigned to the C–H stretching vibration of the methyl (–CH2–) group. These data indicated that the surface of Fe-CDs contained rich hydroxyl groups (–OH), and the benzene ring structure was significantly reduced compared with Fe-TBPP, which showed the characteristic peak of benzene ring at 1601 cm−1.

The absorption spectrum of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1e) exhibited two bands at 235–300 nm, which could be ascribed to π→π * electronic transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. The aqueous solution of Fe-CDs displayed the faint yellow color while emitted strong blue emission under 365 nm irradiation (Fig. 1e). The fluorescence spectrum of Fe-CDs showed a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic. As shown in Fig. 1f, Fe-CDs exhibited a maximum peak at 465 nm under excitation at 380 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the Fe-CDs was measured to be 19.11 % using quinine sulfate as a reference chromophore (Fig. S1).

Furthermore, the chemical composition of Fe-CDs was analyzed by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum revealed the presence of C, N, O, B, and trace amounts of Fe in Fe-CDs (Fig. S2a). Two peaks at 284.9 and 286.2 eV were observed in the C 1 s spectra (Fig. S2b), corresponding to C–C/CC and C–N/C–B, respectively. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. S2c) showed three nitrogen species: including pyridinic N at 398.9 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.9 eV, and oxidized N at 405.0 eV [33]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2d) displayed two peaks at 531.4 and 532.0 eV, corresponding to the C–O/O–B and CO groups, respectively. Two peaks at 186.2 and 190.6 eV were observed in the B 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2e), which could be assigned to the B–C and B–O groups, respectively. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. S2f) was fitted into two peaks at 708.4 and 725.0 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. These results suggested the successful doping of Fe as well as the abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of Fe-CDs.

Using TMB as a substrate, the mimetic catalytic activity of Fe-CDs was evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. 2a, Fe-CDs could enhance the oxidation of TMB to produce the blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2, resulting in an obvious absorption peak at 652 nm. However, no obvious color variation was observed in the control group (Fe-CDs + TMB), demonstrating the efficient peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs. Similar to other peroxide-mimetic enzymes, the pH value, temperature, reaction time, and substrate concentration all affected the catalytic activity of Fe-CDs. As displayed in Figs. 2b-2d, Fe-CDs exhibited the highest catalytic activity when the pH value was adjusted to 3.8 upon incubation for 15 min. Furthermore, the peroxidase-like of Fe-CDs gradually increased with the temperature changing from 30 to 55 ℃, while decreased with further temperature elevation. Thus, pH value of 3.8, temperature of 55 ℃, and 15 min of incubation were selected as the optimum conditions for the following catalytic test of Fe-CDs. Subsequently, we studied the influence of the substrate concentration on the activity of Fe-CDs. As indicated in Fig. 2e–2f, the optimum activity of Fe-CDs could be obtained when the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were set as 1600 and 200 μM, respectively.

Moreover, we further investigated the catalytic behavior of Fe-CDs using kinetic theory and obtained the steady-state kinetic parameters. As indicated in Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b, the standard Michaelis-Menten curves was obtained with TMB or H2O2 as substrates. The steady-state kinetic parameters can be calculated by the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk graphs, such as Michaelis constant (K m) and Maximum velocity (V max) (Fig. S3c and Fig. S3d). K m was determined as an indicator for the affinity of the enzyme with substrates. Generally, a low K m typically denoted a high affinity, and vice versa. As indicated in Table S1, the K m values of Fe-CDs for TMB and H2O2 were valued as 0.716 and 0.720 mM respectively, which were lower than those reported nanozymes in the literatures, suggesting that Fe-CDs possessed high affinity with the substrate. Besides, the V max values of Fe-CDs were determined to be 18.13 × 10–8 M s–1 toward H2O2 and 15.05 × 10–8 M s–1 toward TMB respectively. Compared with that of the most reported nanozymes, Fe-CDs showed higher V max values, indicating its higher catalytic activity.

The above studies indicated a strong correlation between the simulated enzyme activity of Fe-CDs and H2O2 concentration. Thus, Fe-CDs could be used as potential sensor to detect the concentration of H2O2 and related compounds using the absorbance change of ox-TMB. As shown in Fig. S4, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm was linearly related to the H2O2 concentration. The linear correlation between absorbance (A) and H2O2 concentration ([H2O2]) could be expressed as A= 0.000217 [H2O2] + 0.2199 (R2 = 0.9837). Based on this equation, the detection limit (LOD) of H2O2 was determined to be 14.25 μM (S/N = 3). Under aerobic conditions, glucose oxidase can catalyze glucose to produce H2O2 and gluconic acid. Thus, the selective sensor for H2O2 could be used as an indirect platform for glucose detection. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, the absorbance of the “Fe-CDs + TMB + glucose oxidase” system increased with the concentration of glucose (25–500 μM), along with the blue color of solution darkened. The absorbance at 652 nm was linearly correlated with glucose concentration ranging from 50 to 250 μM, with a correlation equation of A= 0.0008016 [glucose] +0.1505 (R2 =0.9955) (Fig. 3b). The LOD was determined to be 3.86 μM (S/N = 3), which was lower than previously reported materials ( Table 1).

To verify the selectivity of the constructed method for glucose detection, we monitored the absorbance changes after adding other interfering substances such as saccharides, common metal ions in serum, and a series of amino acids (Fig. 3c and 3d). Although the content of each interfering substance was 5 times higher than that of glucose, only glucose can cause the significant changes of absorbance, indicating the high selectivity of Fe-CDs for glucose detection. The obvious absorbance change caused by the addition of maltose was probably ascribed to the hydrolysis of maltose to glucose under acidic conditions.

After the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs was gradually reduced. Firstly, we explored the quenching impact of H2O2 toward the fluorescence of Fe-CDs in various pH values and incubation time. As indicated in Fig. S5a, the quenching efficiency of Fe-CDs gradually increased with the pH value and reached the maximum at pH 10.0. In addition, the quenching efficiency was also affected by the incubation time. As displayed in Fig. S5b, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs decreased with the incubation time and remained unchanged after incubation for 5 min. Thus, the following fluorometric tests were performed in buffer solution with pH value of 10.0 after incubation for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs reduced significantly with the glucose concentration ( Fig. 4a). The fluorescence change (∆F) of the system was linearly correlated with the glucose concentration in the range of 20–700 μM, with a correlation equation of ∆F = 0.0003989 [glucose] +0.0543 (R2 =0.9974) (Fig. 4b). The LOD was calculated to be 7.27 μM (S/N = 3), which was obviously lower than that of the previously reported materials (Table 1).

In order to assess the selectivity of the fluorometric method for glucose determination, we monitored the fluorescence changes after adding various other saccharides, common metal ions in serum, and a series of amino acids. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, only glucose can cause the significant change in fluorescence, even adjusting the content of other interfering substance to 5 times higher that of glucose, indicating the high selectivity for glucose detection through the fluorometric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with the visual inspection, the smartphone imaging detection showed the distinct advantages in identification of color changes. Firstly, the smartphone camera can immediately capture a series of visible and fluorescent images. Secondly, the smartphone software (color recognition) could recognize the color of the obtained smartphone images and transform it into H (Hue), S (Saturation), and L (Lightness) values, as shown in Fig. 5a. Finally, the smartphone software (WPS Office) was used to record the HSL value and obtain the linear relationship between HSL value and glucose concentration, where H/L or L/L0 value was selected as the vertical axis and glucose concentration as the horizontal axis (Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c). As depicted in Fig. 5d, the visible blue color gradually deepened with the concentration of glucose, and a good linear correlation between H/L value and glucose concentration (H/L = 0.8597 C + 135.6370, R2 = 0.9934) was obtained, where H (L) referred to the Hue and Lightness values of the smartphone images while C represented the glucose concentration. As displayed in Fig. 5e, with the increase of glucose concentration in the rection system, the fluorescence appears obvious quenching with the color of smartphone imaging picture changing from bright blue to dark blue. The L/L0 displayed a good linear correlation with the glucose concentration (L/L0 = −0.0009463 C + 1.180, R2 = 0.9877), where L0 and L represented the Lightness values of smartphone images under irradiation before and after adding glucose, while C referred glucose concentration. The above experimental results verified the feasibility of smartphone imaging colorimetry for glucose detection, providing a new method for optical dual-mode glucose detection.

The mechanism for the selective detection of H2O2 was investigated by using isopropanol, d-histidine and p-benzoquinone as scavengers, which can capture •OH, 1O2, and O2 •- respectively [41]. As depicted in Fig. 6a, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs was not affected by d-histidine or isopropanol at different concentrations. However, the absorbance at 652 nm of the “Fe-CDs + TMB + H2O2” system was significantly decreased with the addition of various concentration of p-benzoquinone, indicating the type of reactive oxygen species that oxidized TMB was O2 •-. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe-CDs upon H2O2 was evaluated through the above process. As shown in Fig. 6b, when the various concentrations of d-histidine or isopropanol were added to the “Fe-CDs + H2O2” system, the fluorescence quenching still occurred. In contrast, the addition of p-benzoquinone could obviously inhibit the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs, indicating the generated O2 •- was the main specie that quenched the fluorescence. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiment was then carried out to confirm the type of free radicals that produced in the system. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the EPR spectrum of the “Fe-CDs + H2O2+ DMPO” system showed the characteristic signal of DMPO/O2 •- adduct, suggesting the presence of O2 •- [42]. The proposed mechanism for glucose detection of Fe-CDs was summarized in Fig. 6d.

To confirm the practical feasibility of Fe-CDs, the optical dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) method was carried out to detect the glucose content in human serums. As presented in Table 2, the glucose contents in two human sera were calculated to be 5.22 and 4.59 mM respectively based on the colorimetric method of Fe-CDs, which were consistent with the results of fluorometric method (5.16 and 4.59 mM respectively). Moreover, the detection results from the Fe-CDs were almost same with that from the standard glucometer, suggesting the extraordinary potential of Fe-CDs in practical applications.

In brief, we developed a simple hydrothermal approach to fabricate the blue-emissive iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs). The as-prepared Fe-CDs demonstrated remarkable peroxidase mimetic enzyme activity, which can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. Moreover, the Fe-CDs could be utilized to detect H2O2 through the fluorescence “on-off” model. Based on these phenomena, an optical dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) method with high sensitivity and selectivity was established for glucose determination. In addition, we combined this dual-mode method with smartphone imaging to realize the glucose detection with high convenience. The practical feasibility of Fe-CDs for glucose detection was demonstrated in human serum samples using both colorimetric and fluorometric methods. Thus, this study provided a facile strategy to prepare iron-doped carbon dots with strong fluorescence and high peroxidase-mimetic activity, which could be used as a smart nano-sensor for H2O2 and glucose detection via a dual-mode assay.

Ruilin Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Wei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaogang Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2022CFC034), Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientific Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities of Hubei Province: “Biomass chemical technologies and materials” (grant no. T201908), the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (grant no. 2022BEEA06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113125.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
